question.text: The relationship e^(-rT) × F = S is important because:

answer.text: It shows that Black-Scholes (for goods) and Black-76 (for futures) give the same option price at expiry
It proves futures are always overpriced
It applies only to agricultural commodities
It is used to calculate convenience yield

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Since e^(-rT) × F = S, substituting F with S×e^(rT) in the Black-76 formula yields the original Black-Scholes formula. At expiry (T=0), both give identical option prices. **The Trap:** It doesn't prove overpricing. It applies to all commodities. It's not about convenience yield. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV covers the relationship between spot and futures in option pricing. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In practice, Options on Goods may differ from Options on Futures due to volatility differences (spot markets have no DPL) — the mathematical equivalence holds only under identical assumptions.
